30:0.rendering: "These are the words of King Lemuel — a burden his mother pressed into his heart."
30:0.note: The Hebrew word behind "prophecy" is massa, which means a load you carry, not just a message you speak — this mother didn't simply teach her son words, she handed him a weight of love he was meant to bear his whole life, the same way Jesus carries the burden of our formation and never sets it down.

30:1.rendering: "O my son, my own flesh and blood, the child I poured my prayers into!"
30:1.note: A mother's triple cry isn't poetic repetition — it's a heart so full it has to say it three times, and every prayer you've ever prayed over someone you love is an act of faith that God will do with them what you cannot.

30:2.rendering: Do not waste your vigor on women who drain you, nor give your life's path to the vices that have toppled kings.
30:2.note: The Hebrew word for "strength" here is the same word used for a man's firstborn son — his best, his future, his legacy — so Solomon isn't just warning against lust, he's warning against giving away the very thing Jesus came to restore in you.

30:3.rendering: "Listen, Lemuel — kings have no business drinking wine, no business at all, and rulers must stay clear of strong drink."
30:3.note: The Hebrew hammers the prohibition twice with "not for kings" before naming the vice, so the repetition isn't poetic filler — it's a loving mother grabbing her son by the shoulders, and we who belong to the King of kings are called to the same sober, clear-eyed life of service he modeled for us.

30:4.rendering: "Lest they drink and forget what the law demands, and twist justice against those who have no one to fight for them."
30:4.note: Kings were warned away from wine not because pleasure is evil, but because power dulls fastest in those who can no longer hear the cry of the powerless — and Jesus, our true King, drank the cup of suffering so he could hear every cry perfectly.

30:5.rendering: Give strong drink to the one who is dying, and wine to the one whose heart is crushed.
30:5.note: The Hebrew word for "perish" pictures someone already stumbling toward death's door — so this isn't permission to numb life's ordinary aches, but mercy shown to the person at the absolute end of their rope, which is precisely where Jesus meets us.

30:6.rendering: Let him drink and forget his want, and trouble his weary heart no more.
30:6.note: The ancient word for "misery" here carries the weight of labor that breaks the body — this verse isn't celebrating escape from life, but offering mercy to someone already crushed by it, the way Jesus offers rest not to the comfortable but to those who are heavy-laden.

30:7.rendering: Open your mouth for those who cannot speak — stand up for all who are left with no one to fight for them.
30:7.note: The Hebrew pictures someone literally struck mute by powerlessness or fear, and every major translation stream — Aramaic, Greek, Latin — kept that image of a silenced person who desperately needs your voice borrowed on their behalf, which is exactly what Jesus did for us when we had no case to make for ourselves.

30:8.rendering: Speak up — defend what is right, and fight for those who have nothing and no one.
30:8.note: The Hebrew word behind "plead the cause" is the word for a courtroom champion, someone who stands up when you can't stand for yourself — and that's exactly what Jesus did for every one of us, which is why we can't stay silent for others.

30:9.rendering: A truly excellent wife — who can find her? She is worth far more than jewels.
30:9.note: The Hebrew word behind "virtuous" is chayil — raw strength, not just niceness — so this woman isn't simply sweet; she's fierce, capable, and rare, like someone worth searching hard for, the way the gospel invites us to seek the one treasure worth everything.

30:10.rendering: Her husband's heart rests in her completely — he lacks nothing of real value because of her faithfulness.
30:10.note: The Hebrew word for "trust" here is the root of the name Bethlehem means "house of bread" — a place of confident nourishment — and long before Jesus was born there, God was painting a picture of the kind of trustworthy love that makes a home feel like enough.

30:11.rendering: She will bring him good, not harm, every day of her life.
30:11.note: The Hebrew word behind "good" here is the same word used when God looked at creation and called it "very good" — this woman isn't just avoiding cruelty, she's actively pouring life into her husband the way God pours life into the world, and Jesus is the ultimate picture of that kind of relentless, creative goodness toward us.

30:12.rendering: She searches out wool and flax and works with eager, willing hands.
30:12.note: The Hebrew word here isn't passive contentment — she hunts for her materials, the same searching energy we're called to bring when we seek the Kingdom, where Jesus meets every willing hand with purpose.

30:13.rendering: She is like a merchant ship, bringing home nourishment gathered from distant shores.
30:13.note: The Hebrew pictures a woman who refuses to let her household go hungry by accident — she plans, she travels, she provides; and when you see that image clearly, you're looking at a shadow of Jesus, who crossed the greatest distance imaginable to bring us the bread of life.

30:14.rendering: She rises before dawn to feed her family and direct her servants.
30:14.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" carries the idea of a task or assignment — she doesn't just feed her household, she deploys them, and Jesus is the one who rises early for us, giving us both our daily bread and our purpose before we even know we need it.

30:15.rendering: She studies a field carefully before she buys it, then with her own earnings she plants a vineyard.
30:15.note: The Hebrew word beneath "considereth" means she tastes and tests the land — the same kind of wise, unhurried discernment Jesus calls us to before we build a tower or go to war, because a life well-invested begins with knowing what you're stepping into before you step in.

30:16.rendering: She wraps herself in strength and rolls up her sleeves for the work ahead.
30:16.note: The Hebrew picture is a woman cinching her robe tight before hard labor — not flexing for admiration, but preparing for service; Jesus himself "girded" himself with a towel to wash feet, and that same eager readiness for costly, unglamorous work is exactly what this woman embodies.

30:17.rendering: She sees that her work is good, and her lamp burns through the night.
30:17.note: The Hebrew word for "perceiveth" carries the weight of tasting and proving — she doesn't just glance at her work, she savors its goodness the way we are invited to taste and see that the Lord is good; and Jesus, the Light of the World, never sleeps, never lets his lamp go dark over those he loves.

30:18.rendering: She reaches out her hands to the spindle, and her palms grip the distaff.
30:18.note: The Hebrew uses two different words for "hands" — open hands reaching out to take up work, and strong palms gripping it through — because faithful love doesn't just start things beautifully, it holds on when the work gets hard, just as Jesus reached toward us in grace and has never once let go.

30:19.rendering: She opens her hands wide to the poor and reaches out to those in need.
30:19.note: Hebrew uses two different words here — one for the destitute who lack basics, one for the genuinely desperate — so she isn't just generous once; she keeps stretching further, the way Jesus always moved toward whoever hurt most.

30:20.rendering: She has no fear of winter storms, for her whole family is wrapped in warmth and ready.
30:20.note: The Hebrew word translated "scarlet" may actually mean "double-layered" — she didn't dress her people in luxury, she dressed them in enough — and Jesus does the same for us, covering us not just barely but thoroughly.

30:21.rendering: She makes her own bed coverings; her clothes are fine linen and rich purple cloth.
30:21.note: The colors here matter — purple in the ancient world wasn't a fashion choice, it was a statement of worth, and this woman wears it not because someone gave it to her but because she made it herself, which is exactly how Jesus clothes his people: not with borrowed dignity but with righteousness that actually belongs to them.

30:22.rendering: Her husband is respected at the city gate, where the leaders of the community make decisions.
30:22.note: The "gate" wasn't a landmark — it was ancient city hall, and a man's seat there meant his household had earned the town's trust; behind every respected leader, this poem quietly insists, is a wife whose faithful work built something worth respecting.

30:23.rendering: She crafts fine linen and sells it, and supplies sashes to the traders.
30:23.note: This woman doesn't wait for the market to come to her — she goes out and meets it, which is exactly the kind of active, generous, outward-facing love that Jesus calls all of us to live.

30:24.rendering: She is clothed with strength and dignity, and she laughs at the days ahead.
30:24.note: The Hebrew word we translate "honour" carries the idea of weightiness — the kind of person whose presence fills a room — and the word for "rejoice" is the laughter of someone who isn't afraid of what's coming, because she already knows how the story ends; Jesus offers us that same fearless dignity today.

30:25.rendering: She speaks with wisdom on her lips, and the teaching of steadfast love is always on her tongue.
30:25.note: The Hebrew word often translated "kindness" is hesed — God's own loyal, covenant love — and the word for "law" suggests not a rule but a lifestyle of instruction, so this woman doesn't just know how to be kind, she has made it her whole way of living, which is exactly the life Jesus calls every one of us into.

30:26.rendering: She keeps a watchful eye on everything happening in her home, and never wastes a single day.
30:26.note: The Hebrew word for "watches" pictures a lookout scanning the horizon — she's not passive about her calling, and neither was Jesus, who "looked well" to every soul in his path and never wasted a moment on his way to the cross for us.

30:27.rendering: Her children stand and declare her blessed; her husband too rises to sing her praise.
30:27.note: The Hebrew word for "arise" carries the sense of standing up on purpose — like giving someone a standing ovation — so these aren't casual compliments mumbled at the dinner table, but public, intentional declarations of honor, the kind Jesus calls us to give those who quietly lay down their lives for others every single day.

30:28.rendering: "Many women have done noble things, but you surpass them all."
30:28.note: The Hebrew word here isn't just about being good — it carries the weight of a warrior's courage, so this mother isn't simply virtuous in a quiet, polite way; she's been fiercely, sacrificially strong, and her daughter has somehow loved even more boldly — which is exactly how Jesus sees you when you pour yourself out for others.

30:29.rendering: Charm can lie. Beauty fades. But a woman who walks in the fear of God — she is the one worth honoring.
30:29.note: The Hebrew word behind "fear" isn't cowering dread but the kind of reverent love that keeps you oriented toward God the way a sunflower stays turned toward light — and Jesus himself is the fullest picture of a life so anchored.
